author: @PelagicMouthBreather
comment: There’s a point where we have to recognize that we’re in an abusive relationship with these tech companies, even the “nice” ones. Apple already way overcharges for RAM on all their products and their profit margins are the highest in the industry. I’ve defended Apple generally for their premium prices as they make a well integrated product that really lasts a long time (I’ve still got a 2012 iMac), but I’m calling it quits. They can absorb the costs, they’re making record profits, but number must go up, so they twist the knife. At the same time my 17 Pro Max is so jacked up after 26.5.1 that I want to chuck it in the river. Both my personal and work M4 MBPs are flaky as hell now. iPadOS is a joke. The software reliability downward spiral has really become a deal killer for me. iPhones are great until their first update and then it’s a year of frustration. I’m usually a new phone every year guy, but I’m done buying anymore Apple products for a while. I wonder if there’s a lot of people like me and this will hurt their bottom line more than it will help. There’s a point where you even alienate your most loyal  customers. I’m not switching to Android spyware but I’m just going to keep my current phone and Mac for at least another year.

This has nothing to do with memory price increases but Apple being extremely future looking. AI is projected to replace anywhere from 30% to 50% of the white collar workforce by 2030, so far the industry has employment reduction of 15% achieved either by directly replacing existing staff with AI tech or hiring freeze at below senior and expert level roles.

author: @paulharrison8379
comment:
Tim Cooke is not an engineer and it shows in the way that he has ensured that almost every  Apple Silicon Mac sold is misconfigured. By charging $200 for an 8Gb RAM upgrade and $200 for a 256Gb flash upgrade almost every Mac user now has a Mac in front of them that could be 25% faster if he had chaged the $25 cost price for each of these upgrades and had made them compulsory. There was a hint of sanity in MacBook Neo pricing when Apple chose to only charge $100 for a 256Gb flash upgrade.

Apple fanboys will say that MacOs needs less RAM than windows but this is only because with shared memory Macs can make more RAM available for Apps when not playing games. Fanboys will also say who needs more than 256Gb of flash storage. My answer is that any tripple AAA game requires more than 100Gb of storage on your SSD, making a 256Gb SSD unusable for gaming.

Last year I bought a Lenovo Ryzen 5 laptop with 16 Gb of RAM and a 512Gb SSD half price for £399. Lenovo did not skimp on the RAM and flash memory because at that point it was easy to fit lots of both and only add $50 to the price to do this. It also like most Windows Laptops has shared RAM which disproves misstruths spread by Apple fanboys that only Macs have shared memory.

author: @LaymansReviews
comment:
I'm actually happy I stopped buying apple sauce.
Apple product restrictions are to hi, prices are to hi and the company is to high. As in they are all smoking something.
After being away from android for 10+ probably over 11 years, I got sick of apples updates constantly bricking my phones and pads. So now I'm giving android the opportunity to brick my devices.
And so far they have bricked exactly zero.
And here is the better of it. I can get a new entry level android with 6 years support and it still costs less than a used I phone.
Average cost of a Samsung A26 325.00 new. 256gb storage. I have it and it works great. It's not a flagship but it does everything that a normal human being wants to do. Just let it boot all the way up.
Most androids can be set to turn off and reboot at scheduled times. I recommend having it set to reboot no less than once a week. I do it daily.
So my phone has reboot and reloaded by the time I wake up.
A used iPhone 12ProMax with 256 is the nearest to price for storage. That I can find on Amazon.
So for the cost, the 12PM is expected to get one more year of full OS updates and probably another few of security updates. This is questionable tho. When I first started using iPhones and Pads I bought only old used and almost all of them were still getting security updates periodically for about 3 years after they stopped getting OS support. When I walked away from apple it was down to maybe 1.5 years.
And for those who don't know any device getting security updates IS a supported device. Technofiles will try to convince you it is when OS updates end but it's not.
So technically the 12PM ends support in 2027, in practice maybe 2029, if apple is very generous. The Samsung A26 has 4.8 years worth of OS/Security updates. The device is a little older that I realized, it comes with expandable memory in the form of a SD card tray and you can decide how much to increase it. Depending on your source it is expandable from 1tb to 2tb. And Ram Plus. Which is a virtual ram system. It's not the same as regular ram, but does improve day to day operation. In my book, the a26 wins this every time.
Expanded anything from a iPhone is a non option unless you count an expanded  price as being a bonus.
And the fact that most androids still have physical sim trays in them makes it a no brainer. Apple and Pixel having gone to forced esim have taken their products off the table for anyone who knows what esim is and why it's a nightmare.

comment: I don't entirely agree with your take; you glossed over the issue of price hikes in electronics—and not just with Apple. A large portion of integrated circuits and raw materials is needed for weapons production, and as you’re likely aware, there are quite a few wars going on around the world. Another point is that the price includes software support and system updates—Apple supports its devices for six years, unlike the two years typical for Android. Consequently, an iPhone has a much longer lifespan and remains less vulnerable to security flaws over that six-year period compared to an Android phone used for the same amount of time.

author: @CorneliusTaylor-s1u
comment:
For the cynical out there, Apple has actually and amazingly kept pricing around the same for well over 20 years, or at least as long as I have been following the company. In some cases the prices were until recently less.

Lets go back 16 years to 2010, when the iPad was introduced. It was $499. Until recently the iPad was $349. The Mac mini started at $699. Until recently it was $599. The iMac started at $1199. Until recently it was $1299. The MacBook Air started at $1299 for the 13" model. Until recently it was $1099. The iPhone was $599. The iPhone 17e is the same price today for a drastically better phone. It is the same story for most of their other products. 

While it is a shame that pricing has gone up, people should keep that in mind. We have had it good for a long time. People are spoiled. The same goes for the costs of storage.

author: @johnaistrup9250
comment: Its nothing but greed on their refurbished!  Made under old pricing but charging the higher Apple Tax regardless.  F*ck Apple! I was going to upgrade my iPhone and a couple of other products in September, now they can kiss my ass! I get that there is a chip shortage, but Apple and their over indulgent price hikes is breathtaking even by their own standards! Everyone was used to paying the Apple Tax, especially on memory and storage as part of their already over-inflated prices but to go this hard on their customers is quite frankly disgusting.  They could have easily absorbed the costs of the price hikes for a several more years until the market had either crashed and burned or prices stabillised, they did NOT need to do this at all! I was a self-confessed Apple fan but these product prices are now well out of bounds for most people, even more so now and I hope that consumers realise this is all just a money grab and once the AI bubble bursts these big tech firms will soon be scrabbling around for our money.  We all know that once the RAM and Storage prices collapse that Apple will never reduce their prices unless consumers boycott them.  I might want to call for a worldwide boycott now but I accept that many techtubers and wealthy individuals will never stop buying Apple even with these disgusting price hikes.  So I am looking elsewhere from now on for my upgrades, secondhand it would appear, but even there Apple has fucked that market by increasing those prices too in their refurbished section.

author: @SnarlTernIcon
comment:
This is not simply Apple “passing on costs”. It is margin protection.

Yes, memory and storage prices have gone up because AI data centres are swallowing supply. That part is credible. But raising prices on refurbished machines that are already built, already stocked, and use components bought before the current squeeze tells you what this really is.

Apple is not pricing each product against its actual cost. It is lifting the whole ladder so new, refurbished and ageing products all protect the same premium margin.

That is why even old products near the end of their cycle get dragged up. Not because an Apple TV from 2022 suddenly costs more to manufacture today, but because Apple does not want any part of its range looking like value.

This is what pricing power looks like when a company knows its customers are locked into the ecosystem.

Cost pressure may be the excuse. Margin protection is the strategy.

There was a hint of sanity in MacBook Neo pricing when Apple chose to only charge $100 for a 256Gb flash upgrade.

Apple fanboys will say that MacOs needs less RAM than windows but this is only because with shared memory Macs can make more RAM available for Apps when not playing games. Fanboys will also say who needs more than 256Gb of flash storage. My answer is that any tripple AAA game requires more than 100Gb of storage on your SSD, making a 256Gb SSD unusable for gaming.

Last year I bought a Lenovo Ryzen 5 laptop with 16 Gb of RAM and a 512Gb SSD half price for £399. Lenovo did not skimp on the RAM and flash memory because at that point it was easy to fit lots of both and only add $50 to the price to do this. It also like most Windows Laptops has shared RAM which disproves misstruths spread by Apple fanboys that only Macs have shared memory.

author: @Ampere291
comment:
what people dont understand is apple has been eating inflation for customers for years now, barely increasing prices on their products, providing more value for refreshes, while the rest of the market exploded. Not only that but their computers are especially valuable because they can be used as "data centers", meaning the demand for them is only rising. Not from your average consumer, but from other companies. Apple cant eat the cost for those when most of the purchases will never buy an apple subscription or any other devices in the ecosystem.

Same story with the steam machine. If It can be bought en mass by other companies, valve will never see a cent of revenue from those purchases, meaning they have to sell the device at cost, and cannot subsidize it with software purchases like a console can. The steam machine is sold at cost of production, it has 8gb of vram, and 16gb of ram, with 512gb of storage. These MacBook's come with 16gb of ram, 512gb of storage, a much more expensive processor, a screen, a keyboard, a battery, and very expensive build quality, along with other components. Of course its going to cost $1800. Add a 200$ margin for apple, (its probably alot less) and 600$ for the rest of the components.

As a 38 year veteran of the Semiconductor manufacturing industry (field engineering, ALD, PEALD), It’s important to distinguish between a physical shortage and a strategic shift in manufacturing priority. 

We aren't seeing a lack of capacity; we're seeing a pivot. Manufacturers are aggressively shifting wafer allocation toward High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) for AI, which consumes roughly 3x the wafer space of standard consumer DRAM. 

This effectively chokes the supply of consumer components, creating an 'artificial shortage' that ‘socializes’ these massive price hikes. If you can get it delivered overnight, the math says it’s a pricing strategy, not a scarcity issue.  

The 'shortage' narrative is being used to socialize higher margins.  (And seems to be working well as people are still purchasing). 

Don't mistake a shift in priority for a lack of supply. A company like SK Hynix (just one of the top tier players in memory) processes in the neighborhood of 550,000 DRAM wafers per month. They aren't running out of capacity; they are just choosing to spend it on HBM (High Bandwidth Memory), which eats up 3x the wafer space of standard DDR5. 

This creates an artificial scarcity that justifies these massive price hikes for consumer goods. If it’s available for overnight delivery, the math says it’s a pricing strategy, not a shortage (though again, I haven’t seen anyone out of stock yet)

 (Note here the recent ‘Per employee’ bonus’s this year for Hynix and Samsung of over 300k/employee….figures since I retired in January.  That’s not ‘shortage’, that’s pure profit)

As for Apple, well, their full shift to TSMC a few years back, I would have to believe they have their pricing and capacity locked up for a few more years, but obviously are either reaching the end of their booked contracts or they are just jumping on the price leveraging bandwagon.

Just my honest opinion….

author: @Ampere291
comment:
what people dont understand is apple has been eating inflation for customers for years now, barely increasing prices on their products, providing more value for refreshes, while the rest of the market exploded. Not only that but their computers are especially valuable because they can be used as "data centers", meaning the demand for them is only rising. Not from your average consumer, but from other companies. Apple cant eat the cost for those when most of the purchases will never buy an apple subscription or any other devices in the ecosystem.

Same story with the steam machine. If It can be bought en mass by other companies, valve will never see a cent of revenue from those purchases, meaning they have to sell the device at cost, and cannot subsidize it with software purchases like a console can. The steam machine is sold at cost of production, it has 8gb of vram, and 16gb of ram, with 512gb of storage. These MacBook's come with 16gb of ram, 512gb of storage, a much more expensive processor, a screen, a keyboard, a battery, and very expensive build quality, along with other components. Of course its going to cost $1800. Add a 200$ margin for apple, (its probably alot less) and 600$ for the rest of the components.

author: @Ampere291
comment:
what people dont understand is apple has been eating inflation for customers for years now, barely increasing prices on their products, providing more value for refreshes, while the rest of the market exploded. Not only that but their computers are especially valuable because they can be used as "data centers", meaning the demand for them is only rising. Not from your average consumer, but from other companies. Apple cant eat the cost for those when most of the purchases will never buy an apple subscription or any other devices in the ecosystem.

Same story with the steam machine. If It can be bought en mass by other companies, valve will never see a cent of revenue from those purchases, meaning they have to sell the device at cost, and cannot subsidize it with software purchases like a console can. The steam machine is sold at cost of production, it has 8gb of vram, and 16gb of ram, with 512gb of storage. These MacBook's come with 16gb of ram, 512gb of storage, a much more expensive processor, a screen, a keyboard, a battery, and very expensive build quality, along with other components. Of course its going to cost $1800. Add a 200$ margin for apple, (its probably alot less) and 600$ for the rest of the components.
